title: Rewriting our Rust WASM Parser in TypeScript
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-23, Reading time: 8 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.openui.com%2Fblog%2Frust-wasm-parser%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d1a64a6f4-e619f94b-5c3a-44e3-846b-d46a2025ce6d-000000/e0AX6fV32d6kjy0B29pQUf4uhJDINA8rpZ9ciDyqaqg=449
summary: Rewriting our Rust WASM Parser in TypeScript (8 minute read) The OpenUI team built their parser in Rust/WASM, but experienced the "WASM Boundary Tax": the overhead of copying data between JavaScript and WASM. After trying multiple solutions, they rewrote the entire parser in TypeScript and implemented an O(N) statement-level incremental caching system to improve streaming performance over the prior O(N²) re-parsing approach.

title: How we give every user SQL access to a shared ClickHouse cluster
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-23, Reading time: 18 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Ftrigger.dev%2Fblog%2Fhow-trql-works%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d1a64a6f4-e619f94b-5c3a-44e3-846b-d46a2025ce6d-000000/kCvvrv4iiMWvdxgZ8eSnhwmBdYqH6hNL8Z2TAVPd-9Y=449
summary: How we give every user SQL access to a shared ClickHouse cluster (18 minute read) TRQL (Trigger Query Language) is a SQL-style domain-specific language that provides users with secure SQL access to a shared multi-tenant ClickHouse cluster. TRQL queries are parsed into an Abstract Syntax Tree, which undergoes schema validation, automatic tenant isolation, and time restrictions before being compiled into ClickHouse SQL.

title: A Case Against Currying
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-23, Reading time: 8 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Femi-h.com%2Farticles%2Fa-case-against-currying.html%3Futm_source=tldrdevops/1/0100019d1a74668c-d8dd9852-9f21-4302-9374-61e2579f3453-000000/kF-skzIrURxVewroHccuE8F35T5Q_lpsxiPCq0NW4_0=449
summary: A Case Against Currying (8 minute read) Curried functions are elegant and common in functional programming, but their main advantage—partial application—is not unique and can be replicated with other styles using simple syntactic techniques. title: Token Myth
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-23, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Frobonomics.substack.com%2Fp%2Ftoken-myth%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019d1adb4e30-658f331b-b175-4b47-96e8-da27e651f040-000000/vyGciFlBzexUmdUOElkvmJQ7Az3dA_5XXJpmuutJdEo=449
summary: Token Myth (4 minute read) Token spend doesn't matter if tasks aren't being completed. The total tokens consumed per task varies across models. A company that charges double for tokens could still be the cheaper option if its models return the right answer in fewer turns. Instead of using token spend as a KPI, companies should measure cost per successfully completed task.

summary: Security advisory for Cargo (1 minute read) CVE-2026-33056 in the third-party tar crate allows a malicious Rust package to modify permissions on arbitrary filesystem directories when Cargo extracts it during a build. crates.io blocked exploitation on March 13 and confirmed no published crates were exploiting this vulnerability. Users on alternative registries remain exposed until they upgrade to Rust 1.94.1, which is scheduled for release on March 26.

summary: How HubSpot solved the two-date problem (1 minute read) Showing both publish and updated dates can hurt CTR if Google surfaces the older one in search results. This keeps search listings looking fresh while still giving readers context, helping maintain SEO performance.

summary: OS-Level Age Attestation is the Good One (37 minute read) OS-level age attestation offers a privacy-preserving alternative to invasive identity verification. This technical standard enables proactive content filtering while preventing the mass collection of sensitive government IDs and biometric data.

summary: Fast regex search: indexing text for agent tools (28 minute read) Providing text search indexes to fast models creates a qualitative difference for Agentic workflows. The impact is more pronounced in larger Enterprise repositories because grep latency scales with the size and complexity of the code being worked on. Removing the time spent searching the code base provides meaningful time savings and allows for much more effective iteration, particularly when the Agent is investigating a bug.

summary: Auto mode for Claude Code (7 minute read) Auto mode for Claude Code is an alternative to --dangerously-skip-permissions. It allows Claude to make permission decisions autonomously. Auto mode ships with an extensive set of default filters, and users can customize them further with their own rules.

title: Agency
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-25, Reading time: 11 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FjDMTk2/1/0100019d2485775b-614790a6-4cd3-44a0-92c4-c28dc59e78aa-000000/4BFRQErcnClbHzg9SGruZO3lq_pLHuDJgNcQvkrJcrc=450
summary: Agency (11 minute read) Agency is the ability to hold the entire development loop in your head and act on it. A high-agency person can move through the whole sequence without waiting for someone else to hand them the next step. Agency is what you call it when one person can cover the full distance. What matters is that the whole thing exists in a user's hands, and that it was driven there quickly by one person's judgment.

title: How Agentic RAG Works
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-25, Reading time: 12 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fblog.bytebytego.com%2Fp%2Fhow-agentic-rag-works%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d24b03cf4-6c41655b-5ce2-41b1-b45a-f40151fc59af-000000/xJS6aC61ir4otmkeff1TtF_XkB16ybIDZeo2cys9W74=449
summary: How Agentic RAG Works (12 minute read) Standard RAG is a one-shot pipeline: query in, retrieve, generate. However, it breaks down the moment a question is ambiguous, the answer is spread across multiple sources, or the retrieved chunk looks relevant but isn't. Agentic RAG fixes this by adding an LLM in the middle that can rewrite the query, route to the right source, evaluate whether what came back is actually good enough, and loop again if not.

title: Node.js worker threads are problematic, but they work great for us
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-25, Reading time: 13 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.inngest.com%2Fblog%2Fnode-worker-threads%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d24b03cf4-6c41655b-5ce2-41b1-b45a-f40151fc59af-000000/H8sA5AZ9coHJSHxR-fOieVNS6vXa64hieK0ttw4pD-w=449
summary: Node.js worker threads are problematic, but they work great for us (13 minute read) Inngest encountered event loop starvation in its Node.js-based Connect service, where CPU-heavy user code blocked critical heartbeats, causing "no available worker" errors. Their solution was to move the Connect service's internals, like WebSocket connections and heartbeats, into a separate Node.js worker thread to isolate them from user code execution.

This isn't just a feature release—it's Apple stepping into the core IT stack and undercutting Google Workspace and Microsoft 365 on price. The play is clear: own the device, own the management layer, then pull the rest of the stack along with it, starting with smaller companies and moving upmarket.

This marks a shift where IAM becomes the control plane for both humans and autonomous systems.

title: AI deployments aren't failing because of the models - they're failing because of the architecture
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-25, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.salesforce.com%2Fnews%2Fstories%2Fagentic-enterprise-design-principles%2F%3Futm_source=tldrit/1/0100019d24e9f14e-318bba7d-b8c3-4504-aca7-1e5e6ad4fb81-000000/xPQQmdEmF5PaylH5yxGBbwMzSxoN9mOELvy6tHuxIfw=450
summary: AI deployments aren't failing because of the models - they're failing because of the architecture (5 minute read) Salesforce SVP Shibani Ahuja lays out 8 design principles for agentic AI that actually works in production. The blunt thesis: plastering an agent on top of inadequate legacy architecture doesn't work. Governance, modularity, and identity management for agents are non-negotiable from day one.

title: Vibe Coding: 2,000+ Vulnerabilities and the Next Subprime Security Crisis
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-25, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fmvidmar.substack.com%2Fp%2Fvibe-coding-2000-vulnerabilities-ai-software-subprime-security-crisis-2026%3Futm_source=tldrit/1/0100019d24e9f14e-318bba7d-b8c3-4504-aca7-1e5e6ad4fb81-000000/06k7V5gnBFpWQNOOlqu1t5ScwmGJg_f4xwv3TeFcpGs=450
summary: Vibe Coding: 2,000+ Vulnerabilities and the Next Subprime Security Crisis (4 minute read) AI-driven vibe coding is enabling teams to ship software faster than they can understand or secure it, leading to widespread issues like exposed secrets, broken auth, and vulnerable code. The risk isn't the models - it's teams deploying code they don't fully review, creating a growing backlog of hidden security debt that could surface at scale.
